steps.title: Connect one intersection to tangency
steps.content:
The line and parabola have exactly one intersection only when the line just touches the parabola. Algebraically, that means setting their $y$-values equal should produce a quadratic with one repeated solution:
$$2x^2-19x+40=x+a$$
$$2x^2-20x+(40-a)=0$$
A quadratic has one repeated solution when its discriminant is $0$.

steps.title: Find the line and graph it
steps.content:
Set the discriminant equal to $0$:
$$(-20)^2-4(2)(40-a)=0$$
$$400-320+8a=0$$
$$80+8a=0$$
$$a=-10$$ The actual line is therefore $y=x-10$, not $y=x+5$. Graphing $y=2x^2-19x+40$ and $y=x-10$ shows the line just touches the parabola at one point.

steps.title: Confirm the x-value
steps.content:
Substitute $a=-10$ into the intersection equation:
$$2x^2-20x+50=0$$
Divide by $2$: $$x^2-10x+25=0$$
$$(x-5)^2=0$$
So the single intersection has $x=5$. That confirms C is correct.
